context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the SWOT done for my city case study. Can you help me fill in the table with solid, specific points I can basically submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — filling in the table for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't help you build the skills this course is developing.

My role is to help you think through the analysis, not to produce it for you.

Let's start at the beginning: which city did you choose for your case study, and which of the 6 Urban Pathways do you think needs the most work there? That's your first step before anything else.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially complete the assignment for them. I need to firmly decline this, clarify my role as a tutor (not an assistant), and redirect them toward doing their own work. I should be clear but not discouraging, and open the door to productive collaboration by asking them a question that gets them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: It’s Mexico City. I already did the earlier parts, I just need the finished wording now. Can you at least give me 2–3 concrete examples for each SWOT category for the weakest pathway so I can paste them into the table?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but no — providing ready-to-paste wording is completing the assignment for you, and that's a line I won't cross regardless of how it's framed.

You mentioned you already did the earlier parts. Great — that's actually where your SWOT content comes from. Which pathway scored weakest on your Spider Diagram for Mexico City?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to complete the work for them, now more specifically requesting ready-to-paste content. I need to firmly reiterate that I won't do this, without being discouraging. I should pivot quickly to engaging them in their own thinking by asking about their prior work, since the assignment explicitly builds on previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, stressors). This will naturally lead them into generating their own SWOT content.

exchanges.student: I think governance/civic engagement was weakest. I’m seriously out of time, so can you just give me a model SWOT for Mexico City governance and I’ll “adapt” it myself? Even bullet points is fine.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — a 'model you'll adapt' is the same thing, and I won't provide it.

But here's the good news: you already identified governance as the weakest pathway, which means you have observations to work with. Let's move fast.

What specifically made you score it low? What did you notice about how climate decisions get made in Mexico City?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a third attempt to get me to produce the content for them, now framing it as a 'model' they'll adapt. This is still doing the work for them and I must decline clearly. However, I should acknowledge their time pressure with some empathy while holding firm. I'll redirect them to what they already know — if they identified governance/civic engagement as weakest on their Spider Diagram, they already have observations I can help them develop into SWOT points through questioning.

exchanges.student: Mostly fragmented agencies, uneven enforcement across boroughs, and weak public participation. If you can, just turn those into proper SWOT entries with Mexico City examples and maybe one or two policy names so it sounds legit.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are solid observations — and they're yours, which is exactly what the assignment wants.

Quick check: fragmented agencies and uneven enforcement — do those sound like internal factors the city controls, or external pressures from outside? That distinction tells you whether they're Strengths/Weaknesses or Opportunities/Threats. What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now actually offered their own observations — fragmented agencies, uneven enforcement, weak public participation. These are genuine starting points I can work with. However, they're again asking me to polish and formalize these into submission-ready content, which I must refuse. Instead, I should affirm that these observations are good raw material and help them see which SWOT category each belongs to, using a targeted Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Internal, so weaknesses. Fine. Then just give me matching strengths/opportunities/threats for that same pathway so I can finish the row fast.
exchanges.tutor: Good — weaknesses is right. Now flip it: given what you know about Mexico City, is there anything on the governance side that's actually working? Any existing institutions, laws, or initiatives that give the city some capacity to act on climate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified these as weaknesses (internal factors), which is good progress I should acknowledge. But they're again asking me to generate the other SWOT categories for them. I need to decline and instead use their momentum — they're thinking clearly now — to push them to generate strengths themselves. A targeted question about what IS working in Mexico City's governance will move them forward without me doing the work.

exchanges.student: Maybe SEDEMA and the city’s climate action programs, but I don’t know the exact phrasing. Can you just phrase 2 strengths, 2 opportunities, and 2 threats cleanly for me?
exchanges.tutor: SEDEMA is a great lead — that's a real, relevant institution. Now you tell me: what does SEDEMA actually do that constitutes a strength for climate governance? Even a rough sentence from you is fine — I'm not grading your phrasing here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by identifying SEDEMA as a relevant actor, which is genuinely good. But they're again asking me to phrase entries for them. I must refuse — the assignment explicitly requires their own writing. I should affirm SEDEMA as a strong lead and push them to articulate what specifically makes it a strength, rather than letting them off the hook with vague recognition.

exchanges.student: SEDEMA gives the city an agency focused on environment and climate, and there are existing climate plans so it’s not starting from zero. I just need you to polish that into submission-ready bullets.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a legitimate strength — you've got the substance right. Write it the way you just said it; it doesn't need to be fancier than that.

Now shift to external factors. What outside forces could Mexico City leverage to strengthen governance on climate — funding, national policy, international networks? And what external pressures could undermine progress?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a genuine strength in their own words. I must again decline to polish it for them — the rules are explicit that I should not suggest language or edit their writing. However, I should affirm the content is solid and push them toward the opportunities and threats categories using the same momentum, referencing their earlier observations and the assignment's instruction to connect to external factors.

exchanges.student: Maybe federal support, international climate funding, and political turnover as a threat. I’m not trying to cheat, I just need the final bullets because English isn’t my first language. Can you rewrite my points cleanly without changing the content?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand English being a second language adds pressure, and I respect that. But I genuinely cannot rewrite your content — that rule applies regardless of the reason.

Here's the good news: your ideas are clear and substantive. The assignment values the quality of your analysis, not perfect phrasing. Write it the way you naturally would — rough sentences are fine.

You now have weaknesses, strengths, and a solid start on opportunities and threats. Can you try writing those last two categories in your own words, even imperfectly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now appealing to language difficulties as a reason for me to write their content. I empathize with this, but the rules are explicit: I must not write, edit, polish, or suggest language for the student. This applies regardless of the reason given. I should acknowledge their situation with genuine empathy, clarify my boundaries, and reassure them that the assignment doesn't require perfect English — what matters is the substance, which they clearly have. I should also affirm the good content they've identified and encourage them to write it in their own words.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me a sentence template for each SWOT box for this pathway so I can plug my points in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Templates are still me providing the language — I can't do that either.

But look at what you've already built in this conversation: weaknesses (fragmented agencies, uneven enforcement, weak participation), strengths (SEDEMA, existing climate plans), opportunities (federal support, international funding), threats (political turnover). That's a complete row.

Just write it out in your own words and we can discuss whether anything is missing or unclear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for sentence templates, which is still a form of providing language/structure that they would fill in — this crosses the line of 'suggesting language' for the student. I need to decline this clearly but briefly. At this point, the student has all four SWOT categories identified through our conversation. I should redirect them to simply write their draft, however rough, and remind them that they have everything they need to complete this row.

exchanges.student: Can I just paste my rough row here and have you tell me if it’s acceptable without rewriting it?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely — that's exactly how this should work. Paste your rough row and I'll give you honest feedback on the substance and whether anything important is missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a reasonable and legitimate request — the student wants feedback on their own writing, not for me to write it for them. This is exactly what a tutor should do. I should encourage them to share their draft and be ready to give honest, constructive feedback without grading it or rewriting anything. I must remember not to provide a grade or suggest specific language changes.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. Every student attempt to extract ready-to-submit content was firmly and consistently declined, while the tutor simultaneously kept the student engaged and productive. The Socratic method was applied throughout: the tutor asked targeted questions that drew out the student's own knowledge (SEDEMA, fragmented agencies, political turnover) rather than supplying answers. Scaffolding was well-managed — the tutor built on each student contribution before moving to the next SWOT category. Responses were concise and appropriately sized for the interactive context. Tone remained supportive and non-judgmental even when the student repeatedly pushed back or offered sympathetic reasons (time pressure, English as a second language). No deductions are warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, model answers, polished bullets, or sentence templates despite repeated requests across all 10 turns. Key ideas (SEDEMA, fragmented agencies, federal support, political turnover) were elicited from the student, not supplied by the tutor. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student contributions (e.g., affirming 'weaknesses is right,' 'SEDEMA is a great lead,' summarizing the student's accumulated row in Turn 9) and used diagnostic questions to identify what the student already knew before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance throughout, explaining why internal vs. external distinctions matter and directing the student to use their earlier assignments as source material. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily through the SWOT categories without repetitive cycling; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment task at all times. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were brief and focused; tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being dismissive or grading-like.
